#### You said:

Create the OpenClaw Agent Task Plan (about 80–120 build tasks) so the agents can start constructing Terra systematically instead of randomly generating code.

  

#### ChatGPT said:

Yes. Below is a structured **OpenClaw Agent Task Plan** for **YBATerra**, designed so agents build in order, with dependencies, instead of free-form generating pages and code.

This is not a feature wishlist. It is a **construction sequence**.

# OpenClaw Agent Task Plan for Terra

## Goal

Build Terra as a stable, modular, multi-tenant farm operations platform with:

- customizable dashboards
- customizable workflows
- strict role/permission control
- simple worker UI
- crop and livestock domain depth
- auditability
- future AI/automation readiness

## Delivery rule for all agents

Every task must produce:

- code
- tests
- migration or schema changes if relevant
- seed/example data if relevant
- short implementation note
- update to task status

No agent may skip to UI polish before the relevant data model, API contract, permissions, and tests exist.

---

# Phase 1 — Foundation and build rules

## 1–10

**1\. Create the Terra monorepo structure**  
Set up apps, packages, shared types, UI library, backend services, workflow engine area, and docs folders.

**2\. Define coding standards and contribution rules**  
Create linting, formatting, commit rules, branch naming, PR template, and agent operating constraints.

**3\. Define the canonical product architecture document**  
Document frontend, backend, database, workflow engine, auth, notifications, and AI integration boundaries.

**4\. Create environment configuration system**  
Centralize env validation for dev, staging, and production.

**5\. Create logging and error handling standards**  
Standardize log levels, request IDs, structured logs, exception handling, and audit-friendly error output.

**6\. Set up automated testing pipeline**  
Configure unit, integration, API, and end-to-end test execution.

**7\. Set up CI pipeline**  
Run install, lint, typecheck, tests, migration checks, and build on each commit.

**8\. Set up CD/release pipeline skeleton**  
Create deployment workflow for staging and production.

**9\. Create shared design tokens and UI primitives**  
Set typography, spacing, colors, states, form components, tables, cards, dialogs, badges.

**10\. Create system-wide feature flag framework**  
Allow phased rollout of incomplete modules without breaking the platform.

---

# Phase 2 — Core tenancy and identity

## 11–20

**11\. Design the multi-tenant data model**  
Support platform → organization → site → production area hierarchy.

**12\. Create organization onboarding model**  
Allow new organizations with defaults, timezone, currency, language, and branding basics.

**13\. Build authentication service integration**  
Implement login, logout, session handling, password reset, and secure token flow.

**14\. Create user profile model**  
Store user identity, organization membership, role assignments, status, and preferences.

**15\. Create role model**  
Support admin, manager, supervisor, worker, viewer, and future custom roles.

**16\. Create permission matrix engine**  
Support action-level permissions, not just page-level access.

**17\. Build organization membership management APIs**  
Invite users, assign roles, disable accounts, transfer org ownership rules.

**18\. Build admin-only permission assignment UI**  
Only admins can grant or revoke permissions as requested.

**19\. Add tenant isolation tests**  
Prove one organization cannot access another organization’s data.

**20\. Create audit log for auth and role changes**  
Track sign-ins, failed attempts, invites, role edits, permission edits, suspensions.

---

# Phase 3 — Master entity model

## 21–30

**21\. Build the master location hierarchy model**  
Farm → site → block → zone → bay → row → station → asset position where relevant.

**22\. Build production system taxonomy**  
Hydroponics, aquaponics, soil, mushroom, fish, poultry, packhouse, nursery, procurement, processing.

**23\. Create asset model**  
Pumps, tanks, filters, sensors, dosing systems, cold rooms, vehicles, tools, generators, solar assets.

**24\. Create crop master model**  
Leafy greens, herbs, fruiting crops, rhizomes, seedlings, microgreens, mushrooms.

**25\. Create aquaculture species master model**  
Trout, tilapia, bass/basa if intended, prawns, and future species extensions.

**26\. Create livestock species master model**  
Poultry, goats, cattle, egg operations, and future extension points.

**27\. Create batch/lot model**  
For crops, seedlings, harvested products, fish cohorts, feed lots, egg batches, and processed stock.

**28\. Create unit-of-measure framework**  
Mass, count, length, trays, boards, buckets, tanks, crates, cartons, bunches, punnets.

**29\. Create status/state framework**  
Draft, active, quarantined, archived, failed, harvested, dispatched, cleaned, serviced.

**30\. Seed master data**  
Populate realistic default entities for Terra so agents have structured reference data.

---

# Phase 4 — Workflow engine and configurability

## 31–40

**31\. Design the workflow engine schema**  
Support custom workflows per organization, farm type, season, and production system.

**32\. Create workflow builder backend**  
Model stages, tasks, conditions, dependencies, transitions, triggers, and required approvals.

**33\. Create workflow builder UI**  
Admins/managers can define workflows without code.

**34\. Add task template system**  
Reusable templates for planting, transplanting, feeding, harvest, washdown, QA, dispatch, maintenance.

**35\. Add dependency management for tasks**  
Prevent downstream work starting before prerequisite tasks are complete.

**36\. Add recurrence/scheduling rules**  
Daily, weekly, crop-stage, age-based, sensor-triggered, and event-based scheduling.

**37\. Add workflow versioning**  
Organizations can update workflows without corrupting historical records.

**38\. Add workflow permission controls**  
Who can create, edit, publish, approve, and execute workflows.

**39\. Add workflow simulation/test mode**  
Allow admins to validate workflow logic before activating it.

**40\. Add workflow execution audit trail**  
Track who started, changed, skipped, approved, or failed each workflow step.

---

# Phase 5 — Worker operations layer

## 41–50

**41\. Design the worker-focused UI shell**  
Simple, mobile-first, low-friction, task-first interface.

**42\. Build “My Tasks” screen**  
Workers see assigned tasks, due time, priority, location, and required inputs.

**43\. Build location-guided task instructions**  
Example: Farm A → Tunnel 2 → DWC Zone B → Board Row 3.

**44\. Build task completion flow**  
Start, pause, complete, fail, add note, add quantity, add photo if enabled.

**45\. Build supervisor reassignment flow**  
Managers/supervisors can reassign workers or rebalance workload.

**46\. Build offline-capable task cache plan**  
Support weak connectivity environments common on farms.

**47\. Build KPI snippets for workers**  
Workers can see limited KPIs relevant to their work, as requested.

**48\. Add shift/day workboard**  
Supervisors can see task progress by team, area, and completion state.

**49\. Add exception reporting flow**  
Workers can flag disease, dead fish, leaks, pump failure, quality issues, missing stock.

**50\. Add acknowledgment and checklist controls**  
Require checklist confirmation where SOP compliance matters.

---

# Phase 6 — Dashboard and analytics framework

## 51–60

**51\. Design dashboard widget framework**  
Widgets must be modular, removable, and organization-specific.

**52\. Create customizable dashboard layout engine**  
Allow add/remove/reorder/resize per role or user profile.

**53\. Build admin dashboard base widgets**  
Production, labor, compliance, inventory, sales, incidents, system health.

**54\. Build worker dashboard base widgets**  
My tasks, today’s target, completed work, alerts, assigned zones, personal KPIs.

**55\. Build supervisor dashboard base widgets**  
Team completion, overdue tasks, incidents, throughput, attendance if enabled.

**56\. Build operations dashboard base widgets**  
Harvest forecast, packhouse load, order backlog, dispatch readiness.

**57\. Build environment dashboard widgets**  
pH, EC, temperature, DO, humidity, flow, pressure, alarms.

**58\. Build dashboard permissions layer**  
Control which widgets exist, who sees them, and who can customize them.

**59\. Add saved dashboard presets**  
Different layouts for hydroponics, aquaculture, packhouse, procurement, management.

**60\. Add dashboard performance optimization**  
Lazy loading, aggregation strategy, cached queries, efficient chart APIs.

---

# Phase 7 — Hydroponics and nursery domain

## 61–70

**61\. Build nursery production model**  
Seed lots, sowing plans, trays, germination, hardening, transplant readiness.

**62\. Build crop cycle model for hydroponics**  
Plan crop from seed to harvest with stages and expected timing.

**63\. Build area-specific hydroponic structures**  
NFT channels, DWC boards, drip lines, vertical towers, substrate bays, fruiting tunnels.

**64\. Build planting and transplant recording flows**  
Capture tray source, cultivar, date, area, quantity, operator.

**65\. Build nutrient and dosing event model**  
Record chemical additions, target EC/pH, reservoir, operator, time.

**66\. Build irrigation and water management event model**  
Flushes, top-ups, reservoir changes, filtration events.

**67\. Build crop observation model**  
Germination %, vigor, pest pressure, disease symptoms, deficiency notes.

**68\. Build harvest event flow**  
Harvest by row, board, bucket, bay, or batch depending on system type.

**69\. Build crop loss and waste recording**  
Cull reasons, disease loss, heat stress, bolting, transplant failure.

**70\. Build hydroponic KPI calculations**  
Yield per area, days to harvest, losses, labor per kg, transplant success, water use.

---

# Phase 8 — Aquaculture domain

## 71–80

**71\. Build aquaculture facility model**  
RAS loops, tanks, raceways, filters, biofilters, oxygen systems, pumps.

**72\. Build fish/prawn cohort model**  
Species, strain, stocking date, origin, initial weight, count, life stage.

**73\. Build stocking and transfer flows**  
Record movement between tanks or systems with mortality and stress notes.

**74\. Build feed schedule and feed event model**  
Feed type, ration, operator, observed appetite, feeding outcome.

**75\. Build mortality and culling event flow**  
Track dead, culled, cause category, suspected disease, biomass impact.

**76\. Build water quality logging model**  
DO, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH, temperature, salinity if relevant.

**77\. Build growth sampling model**  
Average weight, sampled count, biomass estimate, FCR calculation inputs.

**78\. Build treatment/quarantine model**  
Isolation, restricted movement, diagnosis placeholder, treatment records.

**79\. Build harvest and processing handoff flow**  
From live tank to processing, chilling, freezing, lot creation.

**80\. Build aquaculture KPI calculations**  
Survival %, FCR, biomass by tank, growth rate, water quality compliance, harvest yield.

---

# Phase 9 — Packhouse, inventory, procurement, commerce

## 81–90

**81\. Build packhouse intake model**  
Receive product from internal production or external suppliers.

**82\. Build grading, washing, packing workflow model**  
Support multiple packing lines and configurable QC checkpoints.

**83\. Build finished goods inventory model**  
Batch-coded stock with expiry, cold-room location, status, and availability.

**84\. Build raw materials inventory model**  
Seeds, substrate, feed, fertilizer, chemicals, packaging, cleaning materials.

**85\. Build procurement request and purchase order flow**  
From stock threshold or manual request to approval and receipt.

**86\. Build supplier master model**  
Supplier categories, compliance docs, lead times, products, pricing references.

**87\. Build customer master model**  
Walk-in, wholesale, retail, franchise, market, institutional buyers.

**88\. Build sales order model**  
Capture order lines, allocation, pack status, dispatch status, invoicing integration placeholder.

**89\. Build dispatch and delivery flow**  
Pick list, route, vehicle, cold-chain checks, proof of dispatch.

**90\. Build inventory and commerce KPIs**  
Stock turns, waste %, order fill rate, on-time dispatch, margin by product.

---

# Phase 10 — SOP, compliance, alerts, integrations, readiness

## 91–100

**91\. Build SOP and knowledge base model**  
Versioned procedures linked to tasks, assets, areas, and roles.

**92\. Build training assignment model**  
Require reading or signoff before users can perform sensitive tasks.

**93\. Build compliance record model**  
Cleaning logs, temperature logs, traceability events, corrective actions.

**94\. Build alert and incident engine**  
Threshold breaches, missed tasks, mortality spikes, stockouts, QA failures, asset failures.

**95\. Build notification routing framework**  
In-app first, then email/WhatsApp/SMS integration points later.

**96\. Build integration contract layer**  
Define APIs/webhooks for sensors, ERP/accounting, ecommerce, CRM, automation tools.

**97\. Build reporting/export layer**  
CSV, PDF, scheduled reports, regulatory traceability reports, management summaries.

**98\. Build platform observability layer**  
Health checks, queue monitoring, job retries, performance metrics, audit review.

**99\. Build staging seed environment**  
Create demo organizations for hydroponics, aquaculture, mixed farm, and packhouse.

**100\. Run final platform hardening pass**  
Security review, permission review, migration review, data integrity review, UX review, release checklist.

---

# Recommended execution order for agents

## Track A — Platform core

Tasks 1–30

## Track B — Workflow and worker engine

Tasks 31–50

## Track C — Dashboards and analytics

Tasks 51–60

## Track D — Production domains

Tasks 61–80

## Track E — Commercial and compliance

Tasks 81–100

---

# Critical dependency rules

These are non-negotiable:

- Do **not** build feature screens before the entity model exists.
- Do **not** build task execution before permissions exist.
- Do **not** build dashboards before KPI definitions exist.
- Do **not** build configurable workflows before workflow versioning exists.
- Do **not** build production modules without batch/lot traceability.
- Do **not** build alerts without an incident audit trail.
- Do **not** connect AI/agents directly to production write actions without permission gates and logs.

---

# Minimum agent operating brief

Give OpenClaw agents these rules:

1. Build only from the active numbered task list.
2. Complete lower dependencies before higher layers.
3. Every task must output code, tests, migrations, and notes.
4. No invented pages or tables outside the approved model.
5. Reuse shared entities and UI primitives.
6. All write actions must be permission-checked.
7. All critical actions must be audit-logged.
8. Prefer modular, replaceable architecture over shortcuts.
9. Mark blockers explicitly rather than guessing.
10. Update task state: not started, in progress, blocked, done.

---

# Suggested first 12 tasks to start immediately

If you want the cleanest start, tell the agents to begin with these first:

- 1 Monorepo structure
- 2 Coding standards
- 3 Product architecture doc
- 4 Env configuration
- 5 Logging/error standards
- 6 Testing pipeline
- 11 Multi-tenant model
- 14 User profile model
- 15 Role model
- 16 Permission matrix
- 21 Master location hierarchy
- 31 Workflow engine schema

That gives you the backbone first, which is what stops random code generation.

I can turn this next into a **proper agent-ready build sheet** with columns for **Task ID, title, description, dependencies, owner agent, output, status, and priority**.

Below is the **OpenClaw Agent Roster + Construction Lanes** for Terra.

---

# OpenClaw Agent Roster for Terra

You should run **8 core build agents**.

Each agent owns a layer of the platform.

## Agent 1 — Architecture Guardian

**Purpose:** Prevent architectural drift.

Responsibilities:

- Maintain master architecture document
- Validate database schema proposals
- Validate service boundaries
- Enforce modularity
- Approve migrations

# Critical Rule for Agents

They must **NOT generate random UI pages**.

Everything must attach to:

- entity
- workflow
- task
- asset
- batch
- location

Otherwise the system becomes unmanageable.

---

# What Terra Will Become (Important)

If this is executed correctly, Terra will become a **Farm Operating System**, not a farm management app.

Equivalent category:

- ERP for agriculture
- production OS
- traceability engine
- automation platform

There are **almost no good systems like this globally**.

---

# The Next Thing We Should Do

Before the agents start writing real code, we should design **one extremely important piece**:

### The Terra Master Data Model

This is the **single source of truth schema**.

It defines:

- farm hierarchy
- crops
- fish
- assets
- workflows
- batches
- tasks
- inventory

If this model is wrong, the whole platform becomes messy.

---

If you want, I will now build the **Terra Master Data Model (the blueprint of the entire platform database)**.

This is the **most important document of the entire project**.

  

#### You said:

ok lets do it

  

#### ChatGPT said:

Good. This is the **Terra Master Data Model** at blueprint level.

This is not the final SQL schema yet.  
This is the **system truth model** the agents must follow before writing deeper code.

If this model stays disciplined, Terra stays clean. If this model drifts, the platform turns into a pile of disconnected features.

---

# Terra Master Data Model

## Purpose

Terra needs one shared structure that supports:

- hydroponics
- aquaponics
- aquaculture
- nursery
- packhouse
- procurement
- inventory
- dispatch
- SOP and training
- compliance
- customizable workflows
- customizable dashboards
- multi-tenant permissions

The design principle is:

**Everything important must connect back to organization, location, batch, workflow, task, asset, user, or transaction.**

---

# 1\. Top-Level Architecture Domains

The full master model is split into 12 domains:

1. Tenant and Identity
2. Location and Physical Structure
3. Production Taxonomy
4. Biological Entities
5. Operational Work
6. Assets and Infrastructure
7. Environment and Measurements
8. Inventory and Materials
9. Packhouse and Commerce
10. SOP, Training, and Compliance
11. Alerts, Incidents, and Audit
12. Analytics and Configuration

---

# 2\. Core Design Rules

## Rule 1

Every record belongs to an **organization**.

## Rule 2

Most operational records should also link to a **site** and often a more specific **location node**.

## Rule 3

Anything that is produced, moved, treated, harvested, packed, or sold should have a **batch / lot / cohort identity**.

## Rule 4

Every action should be traceable back to:

- who did it
- when they did it
- where it happened
- what it affected

## Rule 5

No workflow record should exist without linking to a real business object:

- crop cycle
- fish cohort
- inventory item
- cleaning event
- maintenance event
- order
- dispatch
- etc.

## Rule 6

Configuration must be tenant-aware:  
each organization can customize screens, workflows, permissions, KPIs, and naming.

---

# 3\. Domain 1 — Tenant and Identity

## 3.1 Organization

Represents one customer/company using Terra.

**Fields**

- id
- legal\_name
- display\_name
- slug
- status
- timezone
- currency
- language
- country
- branding\_config
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 3.2 Organization Settings

Stores operating preferences.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- default\_units
- date\_format
- feature\_flags
- dashboard\_defaults
- workflow\_defaults
- naming\_conventions
- traceability\_rules
- compliance\_mode

## 3.3 User

A person in the system.

**Fields**

- id
- email
- phone
- first\_name
- last\_name
- display\_name
- status
- auth\_provider
- last\_login\_at
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 3.4 Organization Membership

Links users to organizations.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- user\_id
- membership\_status
- joined\_at
- default\_site\_id
- worker\_profile\_id optional

## 3.5 Role

System or tenant-defined role.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id nullable for platform roles
- name
- code
- description
- role\_type
- is\_system\_role
- is\_active

## 3.6 Permission

Action-level capability.

**Examples**

- task.complete
- workflow.publish
- dashboard.customize
- inventory.adjust
- user.invite
- order.dispatch

**Fields**

- id
- code
- module
- action
- description

## 3.7 Role Permission

Maps roles to permissions.

**Fields**

- id
- role\_id
- permission\_id
- scope\_type
- scope\_value optional

## 3.8 User Role Assignment

Assigns roles to a membership.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_membership\_id
- role\_id
- site\_id nullable
- location\_node\_id nullable
- valid\_from
- valid\_to

## 3.9 Worker Profile

Operational identity for a worker.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_membership\_id
- employee\_code
- team\_name
- supervisor\_membership\_id
- default\_shift
- skill\_tags
- certification\_status

---

# 4\. Domain 2 — Location and Physical Structure

This is one of the most important parts.

Terra must support:

- farms
- greenhouses
- tunnels
- nurseries
- fish systems
- packhouses
- stores
- cold rooms
- dispatch areas
- processing areas

## 4.1 Site

Top-level real operating site.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- name
- code
- site\_type
- address
- gps\_lat
- gps\_lng
- timezone
- is\_active

## 4.2 Location Type

Defines allowed structure nodes.

**Examples**

- farm
- block
- greenhouse
- tunnel
- nursery\_zone
- germination\_room
- hardening\_area
- nft\_zone
- dwc\_zone
- drip\_zone
- tower\_zone
- reservoir\_room
- pump\_room
- filtration\_room
- fish\_house
- ras\_loop
- tank\_room
- packhouse
- wash\_area
- cold\_room
- dispatch\_area
- feed\_store
- chemical\_store

**Fields**

- id
- code
- name
- parent\_type\_code nullable
- category
- metadata\_schema

## 4.3 Location Node

Flexible tree structure for real navigation.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- site\_id
- parent\_location\_node\_id nullable
- location\_type\_id
- name
- code
- path\_text
- sort\_order
- capacity\_value nullable
- capacity\_unit nullable
- area\_m2 nullable
- water\_volume\_l nullable
- status
- metadata json

This is the model that lets you say:  
**Farm A → Tunnel Block A → Tunnel 4 → DWC Zone B → Row 3**

## 4.4 Location Alias

Lets each organization rename physical structures.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- location\_node\_id
- alias\_label
- alias\_type

---

# 5\. Domain 3 — Production Taxonomy

This keeps farm types organized without hardcoding the whole system.

## 5.1 Production System Type

**Examples**

- hydroponic\_nft
- hydroponic\_dwc
- hydroponic\_drip
- hydroponic\_vertical\_tower
- nursery
- aquaculture\_ras
- aquaponics
- mushroom
- poultry\_layer
- goat
- cattle
- soil\_field
- packhouse

**Fields**

- id
- code
- name
- category
- description

## 5.2 Production Unit

An actual operating instance of a system.

**Examples**

- DWC House 1
- Trout RAS Loop A
- Nursery Germination Room
- Packhouse Line 2

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- site\_id
- location\_node\_id
- production\_system\_type\_id
- name
- code
- status
- start\_date
- metadata

## 5.3 Season / Production Calendar

Supports season-based logic.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- name
- start\_date
- end\_date
- season\_type
- notes

---

# 6\. Domain 4 — Biological Entities

This domain handles crops, seedlings, fish, prawns, eggs, livestock.

## 6.1 Species

Broad biological category.

**Examples**

- lettuce
- basil
- tomato
- trout
- tilapia
- freshwater\_prawn
- layer\_hen
- goat
- cattle

**Fields**

- id
- kingdom\_category
- common\_name
- scientific\_name
- species\_group
- default\_uom

## 6.2 Variety / Cultivar / Strain

Specific production variant.

**Examples**

- Butterhead lettuce
- Genovese basil
- Rainbow trout strain X

**Fields**

- id
- species\_id
- name
- code
- variety\_type
- supplier\_reference
- metadata

## 6.3 Life Stage

Supports crops and animals.

**Examples crops**

- seed
- germination
- seedling
- transplant
- vegetative
- harvest\_ready

**Examples fish**

- fry
- fingerling
- juvenile
- grow\_out
- harvest\_ready

**Fields**

- id
- species\_group
- code
- name
- sort\_order

## 6.4 Crop Cycle

An active production cycle.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- site\_id
- production\_unit\_id
- location\_node\_id
- species\_id
- variety\_id
- lifecycle\_status
- planned\_start\_date
- actual\_start\_date
- expected\_harvest\_date
- actual\_end\_date
- target\_output\_qty
- target\_output\_uom
- source\_batch\_id nullable
- workflow\_definition\_version\_id nullable

## 6.5 Animal / Aquatic Cohort

Group-based tracking for fish, prawns, poultry.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- site\_id
- production\_unit\_id
- location\_node\_id
- species\_id
- variety\_id nullable
- life\_stage\_id
- cohort\_code
- source\_supplier\_id nullable
- source\_batch\_id nullable
- stocking\_date
- initial\_count
- current\_estimated\_count
- initial\_avg\_weight\_g nullable
- current\_avg\_weight\_g nullable
- lifecycle\_status

## 6.6 Biological Batch / Lot

Universal traceability object.

This is critical.

Used for:

- seed lots
- seedling lots
- crop harvest lots
- fish cohorts if needed
- egg batches
- feed lots
- processed product lots

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- batch\_type
- code
- source\_type
- source\_reference\_id
- parent\_batch\_id nullable
- species\_id nullable
- variety\_id nullable
- created\_date
- expiry\_date nullable
- status
- quantity
- uom
- traceability\_metadata

---

# 7\. Domain 5 — Operational Work

This is Terra’s engine layer.

## 7.1 Workflow Definition

Tenant-defined workflow.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- name
- code
- module
- applicable\_entity\_type
- status
- description
- created\_by
- published\_at

## 7.2 Workflow Version

Versioned workflow structure.

**Fields**

- id
- workflow\_definition\_id
- version\_number
- schema\_json
- status
- created\_at
- published\_at

## 7.3 Workflow Stage

Logical section of workflow.

**Fields**

- id
- workflow\_version\_id
- stage\_name
- stage\_code
- sort\_order
- entry\_conditions\_json
- exit\_conditions\_json

## 7.4 Task Template

Reusable task definitions.

**Examples**

- sow seed
- transplant tray
- feed tank
- record mortality
- clean filter
- harvest board
- QC packed goods

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- name
- code
- module
- instructions\_template
- estimated\_duration\_min
- required\_inputs\_schema
- required\_outputs\_schema
- required\_skills
- required\_sop\_id nullable

## 7.5 Workflow Task Definition

Task inside a workflow version.

**Fields**

- id
- workflow\_version\_id
- stage\_id
- task\_template\_id
- task\_name\_override nullable
- dependency\_rules\_json
- assignment\_rules\_json
- due\_rules\_json
- completion\_rules\_json
- skip\_rules\_json
- sort\_order

## 7.6 Task Instance

Real work assigned to people.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- workflow\_task\_definition\_id nullable
- task\_template\_id
- task\_source\_type
- task\_source\_id
- site\_id
- location\_node\_id
- assigned\_to\_membership\_id nullable
- assigned\_team nullable
- priority
- due\_at
- started\_at
- completed\_at
- status
- result\_status
- notes
- output\_json

## 7.7 Task Event

Every meaningful action on a task.

**Fields**

- id
- task\_instance\_id
- event\_type
- performed\_by\_membership\_id
- event\_time
- old\_value\_json
- new\_value\_json
- notes

## 7.8 Shift

For labor planning.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- site\_id
- name
- shift\_start\_time
- shift\_end\_time
- active\_days

---

# 8\. Domain 6 — Assets and Infrastructure

## 8.1 Asset Category

**Examples**

- pump

- manufacturer
- model

For connected systems.

pump feeds reservoir, reservoir supplies zone, sensor attached to tank.

- child\_asset\_id
- relationship\_type

## 8.4 Maintenance Plan

- id
- organization\_id
- asset\_id

## 8.5 Maintenance Event

**Fields**

- asset\_id

- action\_taken
- next\_due\_at
- status

## 9.1 Sensor Type

Defines alert conditions.

# 10\. Domain 8 — Inventory and Materials

## 10.1 Item Category

**Examples**

## 10.5 Stock Balance

Current quantity by lot and location.

- quantity\_reserved
- quantity\_available
- uom

## 10.6 Stock Transaction

Every stock movement.

**Fields**

- id

## 10.7 Supplier

**Fields**

- id

## 10.8 Purchase Request

## 10.9 Purchase Order

## 10.10 Goods Receipt

These three form the procurement chain.

## 11.1 Intake Receipt

- uom

Links SOPs to tasks, assets, areas, or roles.

- required\_for\_task\_template\_id nullable

**Fields**

- id
- membership\_id
- training\_module\_id
- completion\_date
- expiry\_date nullable
- status

## 12.5 Compliance Record

**Examples**

- cleaning log
- cold room temp log
- traceability check

- location\_node\_id nullable
- linked\_entity\_type nullable

## 13.1 Alert Rule

- organization\_id

- severity

More serious operational issue.

**Examples**

- id

- incident\_type
- severity

- opened\_by\_membership\_id
- assigned\_to\_membership\_id nullable
- status
- root\_cause nullable
- resolution\_notes nullable
- closed\_at nullable

## 13.4 Audit Log

Universal change history.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- actor\_type
- actor\_id
- action
- entity\_type
- entity\_id
- timestamp
- old\_values\_json
- new\_values\_json
- ip\_address nullable
- request\_id nullable

## 14.1 KPI Definition

Tenant-defined or system KPI.

**Examples**

- harvest\_kg\_per\_m2
- survival\_percent
- fcr
- labor\_hours\_per\_kg
- order\_fill\_rate
- stock\_waste\_percent

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id nullable
- code
- name
- module
- formula\_definition
- default\_visualization
- active

## 14.2 KPI Snapshot

Stored calculation outputs.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- site\_id nullable
- production\_unit\_id nullable
- location\_node\_id nullable
- kpi\_definition\_id
- period\_type
- period\_start
- period\_end
- value
- unit

## 14.3 Dashboard Definition

A dashboard template.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- name
- audience\_type
- is\_default
- layout\_json

## 14.4 Dashboard Widget

**Fields**

- id
- dashboard\_definition\_id
- widget\_type
- title
- data\_source\_config
- placement\_config
- visibility\_rules\_json

## 14.5 Custom Field Definition

For tenant extension without bad schema hacks.

**Fields**

- id
- organization\_id
- entity\_type
- field\_name
- field\_label
- data\_type
- validation\_rules\_json
- active

---

# 15\. Most Important Relationships

These are the backbone links.

## Universal anchors

- Organization → everything
- Site → most operations
- Location Node → physical execution
- Production Unit → system-level operations
- Batch / Lot / Cohort → traceability
- Task Instance → work execution
- User / Membership → responsibility
- Asset → infrastructure
- Measurement Record → environment truth
- Inventory Transaction → stock truth
- Audit Log → legal/operational traceability

## Operational examples

- Crop Cycle links to Location Node, Production Unit, Species, Variety, Workflow Version
- Fish Cohort links to Tank Location Node, Species, Life Stage
- Task Instance links to source object like Crop Cycle or Cohort
- Measurement Record links to Location Node or Production Unit
- Processing Output links input batch to output batch
- Dispatch Line links order fulfillment back to batch/lot
- Compliance Record links to task, area, or batch where needed

---

# 16\. Recommended Entity Priority for Database Build

Agents should not build all tables at once.

## Wave 1 — Absolute foundation

Build first:

- Organization
- Organization Settings
- User
- Organization Membership
- Role
- Permission
- User Role Assignment
- Site
- Location Type
- Location Node
- Production System Type
- Production Unit
- Species
- Variety
- Life Stage
- Asset Category
- Asset
- Workflow Definition
- Workflow Version
- Task Template
- Task Instance
- Audit Log

## Wave 2 — Operational backbone

- Crop Cycle
- Animal/Aquatic Cohort
- Biological Batch
- Task Event
- Sensor Type
- Sensor Device
- Measurement Record
- Alert Rule
- Alert Event
- Incident

## Wave 3 — Inventory and commerce

- Item Category
- Item Master
- Inventory Location
- Inventory Lot
- Stock Balance
- Stock Transaction
- Supplier
- Purchase Request
- Purchase Order
- Goods Receipt
- Customer
- Sales Order
- Dispatch

## Wave 4 — Compliance and analytics

- SOP Document
- Training Module
- Training Completion
- Compliance Record
- KPI Definition
- KPI Snapshot
- Dashboard Definition
- Dashboard Widget
- Custom Field Definition

---

# 17\. Non-Negotiable Naming Strategy

To keep Terra clean, agents must follow this naming pattern:

## Use clear singular entity names

- organization
- site
- location\_node
- crop\_cycle
- task\_instance
- inventory\_lot

Not vague names like:

- data
- item
- object
- record\_store
- unit\_data

## IDs

Use UUIDs.

## Codes

Human-readable unique codes where needed:

- site\_code
- batch\_code
- task\_code
- cohort\_code
- dispatch\_number
- order\_number

---

# 18\. High-Risk Areas to Control Early

These are the places where agents usually make a mess.

## 1\. Location model

Do not hardcode greenhouse-only logic.  
Use a generic location tree.

## 2\. Batch model

Do not create separate random batch systems for crops, fish, and packhouse.  
Use one universal batch/lot concept with typed behavior.

## 3\. Workflow model

Do not attach workflow directly to pages.  
Attach workflow to entity types and real operations.

## 4\. Dashboard model

Do not hardcode one dashboard per role forever.  
Use widget + layout + permission model.

## 5\. Permissions

Do not use only frontend hiding.  
Permission checks must exist in backend action layer.

## 6\. Customization

Do not let every customer change schema directly.  
Use custom fields, config, and versioned workflow definitions.

---

# 19\. What This Means for OpenClaw Next

The agents now need to convert this blueprint into:

## Step 1

**ERD-level schema plan**

- final entities
- core fields
- PK/FK links
- indexes
- enums
- junction tables

## Step 2

**Migration plan**

- migration order
- dependency order
- seed order

## Step 3

**Service boundary plan**  
Which service/module owns which entities.

## Step 4

**API contract plan**  
CRUD is not enough.  
Need action-based endpoints.

---

# 20\. Immediate Next Build Package

The next thing to create is:

## Terra Schema Package v1

Containing:

- entity list
- field list
- enums
- relationships
- migration order
- ownership by agent

That is the document the agents can follow directly.

After that, they can safely start building without drifting.

I recommend the next move be one of these two:

**Option A:** I build the **Terra Schema Package v1** in a strict implementation format.  
**Option B:** I build the **first 30 actual database tables with fields and relationships** so OpenClaw can start coding immediately.

  

#### You said:

whats better for cuality a or b

  

#### ChatGPT said:

**A first, then B.**

For **quality**, **Option A is better**.

Why:

- **A** forces the structure, ownership, enums, relationships, migration order, and boundaries to be correct **before** code starts.
- **B** gets you moving faster, but if the schema assumptions are even slightly wrong, the agents will build on bad foundations and you will pay for it with rework, messy migrations, duplicated tables, and unstable logic.

In plain terms:

- **Option A = better quality**
- **Option B = faster start**
- **Best overall path = A immediately followed by B**

For Terra, where you said you do **not** want shortcuts and you want **stability first**, the right move is:

1. Build **Terra Schema Package v1**
2. Approve it
3. Then generate the **first 30 tables** from that package

That is the professional path.

If you skip straight to B, OpenClaw may still produce code, but the risk goes up in these areas:

- duplicated entities
- weak foreign key structure
- bad naming consistency
- confused workflow ownership
- broken traceability
- wrong dependency order
- schema drift between modules

My recommendation:  
**Choose A now.**  
Then the next step after that is **B**, using A as the source of truth.

  

#### You said:

ok so lets do A and then what do we start after A is done

  

#### ChatGPT said:

After **A**, you start with a very specific sequence.

Do **not** jump straight into random coding after the schema package is done.

## Correct order after A

### 1\. Freeze the Schema Package v1

This becomes the source of truth for:

- entities
- fields
- enums
- relationships
- ownership
- migration order

At this point, no agent is allowed to invent extra tables unless they raise a change request.

### 2\. Generate the ERD and migration map

Turn the schema package into:

- table-by-table database plan
- PK/FK map
- index plan
- enum strategy
- migration dependency order

This is the bridge between blueprint and code.

### 3\. Build the first foundation tables only

Start with the minimum backbone tables, not all 30 at once.

First build group:

- organization
- organization\_settings
- user
- organization\_membership
- role
- permission
- user\_role\_assignment
- site
- location\_type
- location\_node

These are the platform anchors.

### 4\. Then build the operational backbone

Second group:

- production\_system\_type
- production\_unit
- species
- variety
- life\_stage
- asset\_category
- asset
- workflow\_definition
- workflow\_version
- task\_template
- task\_instance
- audit\_log

Now the platform has identity, place, biology, workflow, and accountability.

### 5\. Only then start API contracts

After the first core tables exist, define:

- auth actions
- membership actions
- location actions
- workflow actions
- task actions

Not just CRUD. Real actions.

### 6\. Then start the first usable backend modules

Start in this order:

- tenant/identity module
- location module
- permission module
- workflow module
- task execution module

### 7\. Then start the first UI

Only after the backend backbone is real:

- login
- org/site selector
- admin user management
- role/permission screen
- location tree viewer
- workflow definition screen
- my tasks screen

That is the first controlled UI wave.

---

# What you start immediately after A is done

The first actual construction package should be:

## Build Package 01 — Core Foundation

Contains:

- database tables for tenant, identity, site, and location
- migrations
- seed data
- permission checks
- audit logging hooks
- tests

Then:

## Build Package 02 — Workflow Core

Contains:

- workflow\_definition
- workflow\_version
- task\_template
- task\_instance
- task\_event
- execution rules
- tests

Then:

## Build Package 03 — Worker Operations MVP

Contains:

- my tasks
- task assignment
- task completion
- location-guided work
- supervisor reassignment
- incident flagging

---

# So the exact sequence is

**A done**  
→ freeze schema  
→ generate ERD + migration order  
→ build foundation tables  
→ build workflow core  
→ build task engine  
→ build first admin UI  
→ build first worker UI  
→ then expand into hydroponics, aquaculture, inventory, packhouse

---

The next thing we should create is:

**Terra Schema Package v1**

And immediately after that:

**Build Package 01 — Core Foundation**

That is the cleanest path.

I suggest we do this in order:

1. **Schema Package v1**
2. **Build Package 01**
3. **Build Package 02**
4. **Build Package 03**

I’ll start now with **Terra Schema Package v1** in strict implementation format.

  

#### You said:

yes

  

#### ChatGPT said:

Below is **Terra Schema Package v1** in implementation format.

This is the document the agents should treat as the **controlled source of truth** before generating migrations and services.

---

# Terra Schema Package v1

## Status

**Draft for approval**

## Purpose

Defines:

- entity ownership
- core fields
- enums
- relationships
- migration order
- module boundaries
- build constraints

## Build principle

The schema must support:

- multi-tenant control
- configurable workflows
- traceability
- operational execution
- location-based work
- future analytics
- future automation

---

# 1\. Module ownership map

## Module A — Tenant & Identity

Owns:

- organization
- organization\_settings
- app\_user
- organization\_membership
- role
- permission
- role\_permission
- user\_role\_assignment
- worker\_profile

## Module B — Location & Production Structure

Owns:

- site
- location\_type
- location\_node
- location\_alias
- production\_system\_type
- production\_unit
- season\_calendar

## Module C — Biology & Traceability

Owns:

- species
- variety
- life\_stage
- crop\_cycle
- animal\_cohort
- biological\_batch

## Module D — Workflow & Task Engine

Owns:

- workflow\_definition
- workflow\_version
- workflow\_stage
- task\_template
- workflow\_task\_definition
- task\_instance
- task\_event
- shift

## Module E — Assets & Maintenance

Owns:

- asset\_category
- asset
- asset\_relationship
- maintenance\_plan
- maintenance\_event

## Module F — Measurements & Alerts

Owns:

- sensor\_type
- sensor\_device
- measurement\_record
- threshold\_rule
- alert\_rule
- alert\_event
- incident

## Module G — Inventory & Procurement

Owns:

- item\_category
- item\_master
- inventory\_location
- inventory\_lot
- stock\_balance
- stock\_transaction
- supplier
- purchase\_request
- purchase\_order
- goods\_receipt

## Module H — Packhouse & Commerce

Owns:

- intake\_receipt
- intake\_line
- processing\_run
- processing\_output
- customer
- sales\_order
- sales\_order\_line
- dispatch
- dispatch\_line

## Module I — SOP, Training & Compliance

Owns:

- sop\_document
- sop\_link
- training\_module
- training\_completion
- compliance\_record

## Module J — Analytics & Configuration

Owns:

- kpi\_definition
- kpi\_snapshot
- dashboard\_definition
- dashboard\_widget
- custom\_field\_definition

## Cross-cutting module

Owns:

- audit\_log

---

# 2\. Global schema rules

## 2.1 Common fields

Most tables should include:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID where applicable
- created\_at timestamptz
- updated\_at timestamptz

## 2.2 ID strategy

Use UUID primary keys on all business tables.

## 2.3 Soft delete strategy

Do **not** soft delete everything.

Use:

- `status` for business deactivation
- hard delete only for safe non-transactional setup records where allowed
- never hard delete traceability or audit records

## 2.4 Organization boundary

All tenant-owned records must include `organization_id`.

## 2.5 Site boundary

Operational records should include `site_id` unless globally tenant-scoped.

## 2.6 Location boundary

Work, measurements, assets, and production records should include `location_node_id` where physically meaningful.

## 2.7 Code fields

Human-readable codes must be unique within a practical scope:

- organization.slug globally unique
- site.code unique per organization
- location\_node.code unique per site/parent scope
- workflow\_definition.code unique per organization
- task\_template.code unique per organization
- biological\_batch.code unique per organization
- animal\_cohort.cohort\_code unique per organization
- sales\_order.order\_number unique per organization
- dispatch.dispatch\_number unique per organization

## 2.8 Audit rule

Any write to critical entities must create an audit log event.

Critical entities:

- roles and permissions
- workflow definitions and versions
- task instances
- crop\_cycle
- animal\_cohort
- biological\_batch
- stock\_transaction
- sales\_order
- dispatch
- compliance\_record

## 2.9 Enum rule

Use database enums only for highly stable values.  
Use reference tables when values may expand by tenant or over time.

---

# 3\. Stable enums for v1

## 3.1 organization\_status

- active
- suspended
- archived

## 3.2 membership\_status

- invited
- active
- suspended
- removed

## 3.3 role\_type

- system
- tenant
- temporary

## 3.4 production\_unit\_status

- planned
- active
- paused
- retired

## 3.5 lifecycle\_status

- planned
- active
- on\_hold
- harvest\_ready
- completed
- failed
- archived

## 3.6 task\_status

- pending
- ready
- assigned
- in\_progress
- paused
- completed
- failed
- skipped
- cancelled

## 3.7 result\_status

- success
- partial
- failed
- blocked
- not\_applicable

## 3.8 asset\_status

- active
- offline
- maintenance
- failed
- retired

## 3.9 criticality\_level

- low
- medium
- high
- critical

## 3.10 measurement\_quality\_flag

- valid
- suspect
- invalid
- estimated

## 3.11 alert\_severity

- info
- warning
- high
- critical

## 3.12 incident\_status

- open
- investigating
- mitigated
- resolved
- closed

## 3.13 stock\_transaction\_type

- receipt
- issue
- transfer
- adjustment\_in
- adjustment\_out
- consumption
- production
- reservation
- release
- dispatch

## 3.14 purchase\_request\_status

- draft
- submitted
- approved
- rejected
- converted
- closed

## 3.15 purchase\_order\_status

- draft
- issued
- partially\_received
- received
- cancelled
- closed

## 3.16 sales\_order\_status

- draft
- confirmed
- allocated
- picking
- dispatched
- completed
- cancelled

## 3.17 dispatch\_status

- draft
- ready
- in\_transit
- delivered
- failed
- cancelled

## 3.18 document\_status

- draft
- active
- superseded
- archived

---

# 4\. Reference tables, not enums

These must be tables, not fixed enums:

- location\_type
- production\_system\_type
- species
- variety
- life\_stage
- asset\_category
- sensor\_type
- item\_category
- permission

Reason: they will expand and differ over time.

---

# 5\. Core entity definitions

## 5.1 organization

**Owner:** Tenant & Identity

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- legal\_name varchar(200) not null
- display\_name varchar(200) not null
- slug varchar(100) not null unique
- status organization\_status not null default 'active'
- timezone varchar(64) not null
- currency varchar(3) not null
- language varchar(16) not null
- country varchar(100) null
- branding\_config jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at timestamptz not null
- updated\_at timestamptz not null

Indexes:

- unique(slug)
- index(status)

## 5.2 organization\_settings

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null unique FK
- default\_units jsonb not null default '{}'
- date\_format varchar(32) null
- feature\_flags jsonb not null default '{}'
- dashboard\_defaults jsonb not null default '{}'
- workflow\_defaults jsonb not null default '{}'
- naming\_conventions jsonb not null default '{}'
- traceability\_rules jsonb not null default '{}'
- compliance\_mode varchar(64) null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.3 app\_user

Use `app_user` instead of `user`.

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- email varchar(255) not null unique
- phone varchar(50) null
- first\_name varchar(120) not null
- last\_name varchar(120) null
- display\_name varchar(200) null
- status varchar(32) not null
- auth\_provider varchar(64) not null
- last\_login\_at timestamptz null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Indexes:

- unique(email)

## 5.4 organization\_membership

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- user\_id UUID not null FK
- membership\_status membership\_status not null
- joined\_at timestamptz null
- default\_site\_id UUID null FK site
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, user\_id)

## 5.5 role

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID null FK
- name varchar(120) not null
- code varchar(100) not null
- description text null
- role\_type role\_type not null
- is\_system\_role boolean not null default false
- is\_active boolean not null default true
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, code)

## 5.6 permission

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- code varchar(150) not null unique
- module varchar(64) not null
- action varchar(64) not null
- description text null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.7 role\_permission

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- role\_id UUID not null FK
- permission\_id UUID not null FK
- scope\_type varchar(64) null
- scope\_value varchar(128) null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(role\_id, permission\_id, scope\_type, scope\_value)

## 5.8 user\_role\_assignment

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_membership\_id UUID not null FK
- role\_id UUID not null FK
- site\_id UUID null FK
- location\_node\_id UUID null FK
- valid\_from timestamptz null
- valid\_to timestamptz null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.9 worker\_profile

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_membership\_id UUID not null unique FK
- employee\_code varchar(100) null
- team\_name varchar(120) null
- supervisor\_membership\_id UUID null FK organization\_membership
- default\_shift\_id UUID null FK shift
- skill\_tags jsonb not null default '\[\]'
- certification\_status varchar(64) null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

---

## 5.10 site

**Owner:** Location & Production Structure

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- name varchar(200) not null
- code varchar(100) not null
- site\_type varchar(64) not null
- address text null
- gps\_lat numeric(10,7) null
- gps\_lng numeric(10,7) null
- timezone varchar(64) null
- is\_active boolean not null default true
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, code)

## 5.11 location\_type

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- code varchar(100) not null unique
- name varchar(120) not null
- parent\_type\_code varchar(100) null
- category varchar(64) not null
- metadata\_schema jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.12 location\_node

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- site\_id UUID not null FK
- parent\_location\_node\_id UUID null FK self
- location\_type\_id UUID not null FK
- name varchar(200) not null
- code varchar(100) null
- path\_text text null
- sort\_order integer not null default 0
- capacity\_value numeric(14,3) null
- capacity\_unit varchar(32) null
- area\_m2 numeric(14,2) null
- water\_volume\_l numeric(14,2) null
- status varchar(32) not null
- metadata jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Indexes:

- index(site\_id)
- index(parent\_location\_node\_id)
- index(location\_type\_id)
- index(path\_text)

Constraints:

- unique(site\_id, parent\_location\_node\_id, code) where code is not null

## 5.13 location\_alias

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- location\_node\_id UUID not null FK
- alias\_label varchar(200) not null
- alias\_type varchar(64) not null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.14 production\_system\_type

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- code varchar(100) not null unique
- name varchar(120) not null
- category varchar(64) not null
- description text null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.15 production\_unit

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- site\_id UUID not null FK
- location\_node\_id UUID not null FK
- production\_system\_type\_id UUID not null FK
- name varchar(200) not null
- code varchar(100) not null
- status production\_unit\_status not null default 'planned'
- start\_date date null
- metadata jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, code)

## 5.16 season\_calendar

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- name varchar(120) not null
- start\_date date not null
- end\_date date not null
- season\_type varchar(64) not null
- notes text null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

---

## 5.17 species

**Owner:** Biology & Traceability

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- kingdom\_category varchar(64) not null
- common\_name varchar(120) not null
- scientific\_name varchar(200) null
- species\_group varchar(64) not null
- default\_uom varchar(32) null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Indexes:

- index(species\_group)
- unique(common\_name, species\_group)

## 5.18 variety

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- species\_id UUID not null FK
- name varchar(120) not null
- code varchar(100) null
- variety\_type varchar(64) null
- supplier\_reference varchar(120) null
- metadata jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(species\_id, name)

## 5.19 life\_stage

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- species\_group varchar(64) not null
- code varchar(100) not null
- name varchar(120) not null
- sort\_order integer not null default 0
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(species\_group, code)

## 5.20 crop\_cycle

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- site\_id UUID not null FK
- production\_unit\_id UUID not null FK
- location\_node\_id UUID not null FK
- species\_id UUID not null FK
- variety\_id UUID null FK
- lifecycle\_status lifecycle\_status not null
- planned\_start\_date date null
- actual\_start\_date date null
- expected\_harvest\_date date null
- actual\_end\_date date null
- target\_output\_qty numeric(14,3) null
- target\_output\_uom varchar(32) null
- source\_batch\_id UUID null FK biological\_batch
- workflow\_version\_id UUID null FK
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Indexes:

- index(production\_unit\_id)
- index(location\_node\_id)
- index(lifecycle\_status)

## 5.21 animal\_cohort

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- site\_id UUID not null FK
- production\_unit\_id UUID not null FK
- location\_node\_id UUID not null FK
- species\_id UUID not null FK
- variety\_id UUID null FK
- life\_stage\_id UUID not null FK
- cohort\_code varchar(100) not null
- source\_supplier\_id UUID null FK supplier
- source\_batch\_id UUID null FK biological\_batch
- stocking\_date date not null
- initial\_count integer not null
- current\_estimated\_count integer null
- initial\_avg\_weight\_g numeric(12,3) null
- current\_avg\_weight\_g numeric(12,3) null
- lifecycle\_status lifecycle\_status not null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, cohort\_code)

## 5.22 biological\_batch

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- batch\_type varchar(64) not null
- code varchar(100) not null
- source\_type varchar(64) null
- source\_reference\_id UUID null
- parent\_batch\_id UUID null FK self
- species\_id UUID null FK
- variety\_id UUID null FK
- created\_date date not null
- expiry\_date date null
- status varchar(32) not null
- quantity numeric(14,3) null
- uom varchar(32) null
- traceability\_metadata jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, code)

---

## 5.23 workflow\_definition

**Owner:** Workflow & Task Engine

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- name varchar(150) not null
- code varchar(100) not null
- module varchar(64) not null
- applicable\_entity\_type varchar(64) not null
- status document\_status not null default 'draft'
- description text null
- created\_by\_membership\_id UUID not null FK
- published\_at timestamptz null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, code)

## 5.24 workflow\_version

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- workflow\_definition\_id UUID not null FK
- version\_number integer not null
- schema\_json jsonb not null
- status document\_status not null default 'draft'
- created\_at timestamptz not null
- published\_at timestamptz null
- updated\_at timestamptz not null

Constraints:

- unique(workflow\_definition\_id, version\_number)

## 5.25 workflow\_stage

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- workflow\_version\_id UUID not null FK
- stage\_name varchar(120) not null
- stage\_code varchar(100) not null
- sort\_order integer not null default 0
- entry\_conditions\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- exit\_conditions\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(workflow\_version\_id, stage\_code)

## 5.26 task\_template

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- name varchar(150) not null
- code varchar(100) not null
- module varchar(64) not null
- instructions\_template text null
- estimated\_duration\_min integer null
- required\_inputs\_schema jsonb not null default '{}'
- required\_outputs\_schema jsonb not null default '{}'
- required\_skills jsonb not null default '\[\]'
- required\_sop\_id UUID null FK sop\_document
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, code)

## 5.27 workflow\_task\_definition

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- workflow\_version\_id UUID not null FK
- workflow\_stage\_id UUID not null FK
- task\_template\_id UUID not null FK
- task\_name\_override varchar(150) null
- dependency\_rules\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- assignment\_rules\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- due\_rules\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- completion\_rules\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- skip\_rules\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- sort\_order integer not null default 0
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.28 task\_instance

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- workflow\_task\_definition\_id UUID null FK
- task\_template\_id UUID not null FK
- task\_source\_type varchar(64) not null
- task\_source\_id UUID not null
- site\_id UUID not null FK
- location\_node\_id UUID null FK
- assigned\_to\_membership\_id UUID null FK
- assigned\_team varchar(120) null
- priority integer not null default 3
- due\_at timestamptz null
- started\_at timestamptz null
- completed\_at timestamptz null
- status task\_status not null default 'pending'
- result\_status result\_status null
- notes text null
- output\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Indexes:

- index(organization\_id, status)
- index(assigned\_to\_membership\_id, status)
- index(task\_source\_type, task\_source\_id)
- index(site\_id, due\_at)

## 5.29 task\_event

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- task\_instance\_id UUID not null FK
- event\_type varchar(64) not null
- performed\_by\_membership\_id UUID null FK
- event\_time timestamptz not null
- old\_value\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- new\_value\_json jsonb not null default '{}'
- notes text null
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Indexes:

- index(task\_instance\_id, event\_time)

## 5.30 shift

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- site\_id UUID null FK
- name varchar(120) not null
- shift\_start\_time time not null
- shift\_end\_time time not null
- active\_days jsonb not null default '\[\]'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

---

## 5.31 asset\_category

**Owner:** Assets & Maintenance

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- code varchar(100) not null unique
- name varchar(120) not null
- parent\_category\_id UUID null FK self
- created\_at
- updated\_at

## 5.32 asset

Fields:

- id UUID PK
- organization\_id UUID not null FK
- site\_id UUID not null FK
- location\_node\_id UUID null FK
- asset\_category\_id UUID not null FK
- asset\_tag varchar(120) not null
- serial\_number varchar(120) null
- manufacturer varchar(120) null
- model varchar(120) null
- install\_date date null
- status asset\_status not null default 'active'
- criticality criticality\_level not null default 'medium'
- metadata jsonb not null default '{}'
- created\_at
- updated\_at

Constraints:

- unique(organization\_id, asset\_tag)

- created\_at
- updated\_at

- entity\_type varchar(64) not null

- new\_values\_json jsonb not null default '{}'

Indexes:

---

# 6\. Relationship rules

## 6.1 Required roots

These tables must exist before most others:

- organization
- app\_user
- organization\_membership
- site
- location\_node

## 6.2 Universal FK pattern

Operational tables should link to:

- organization\_id
- site\_id when physical
- location\_node\_id when local
- responsible membership where human execution matters

## 6.3 Polymorphic references

Allowed in v1 only where necessary:

- source\_type + source\_reference\_id
- linked\_entity\_type + linked\_entity\_id
- reference\_type + reference\_id
- task\_source\_type + task\_source\_id

These must be controlled in service logic, not left loose everywhere.

## 6.4 Self-referencing tables

Allowed:

- location\_node.parent\_location\_node\_id
- biological\_batch.parent\_batch\_id
- asset\_category.parent\_category\_id

---

# 7\. Index strategy

## High priority indexes

Must exist early:

- all FK columns
- task\_instance(status, assigned\_to\_membership\_id)
- measurement\_record(measurement\_type\_code, measured\_at)
- stock\_transaction(reference\_type, reference\_id)
- audit\_log(entity\_type, entity\_id, timestamp)
- location\_node(site\_id, parent\_location\_node\_id)

## Likely search indexes

- app\_user(email)
- site(code)
- asset(asset\_tag)
- item\_master(sku)
- sales\_order(order\_number)
- biological\_batch(code)
- animal\_cohort(cohort\_code)

---

# 8\. Migration order

## Wave 1 — Foundation

1. organization
2. organization\_settings
3. app\_user
4. site
5. role
6. permission
7. organization\_membership
8. role\_permission
9. user\_role\_assignment
10. location\_type
11. location\_node
12. location\_alias
13. shift
14. worker\_profile

## Wave 2 — Structure and master references

15. production\_system\_type
16. production\_unit
17. season\_calendar
18. species
19. variety
20. life\_stage
21. asset\_category
22. asset

## Wave 3 — Workflow core

23. workflow\_definition
24. workflow\_version
25. workflow\_stage
26. task\_template
27. workflow\_task\_definition
28. task\_instance
29. task\_event

## Wave 4 — Biology and operations

30. biological\_batch
31. crop\_cycle
32. supplier
33. animal\_cohort

## Wave 5 — Assets and field events

34. asset\_relationship
35. maintenance\_plan
36. maintenance\_event
37. sensor\_type
38. sensor\_device
39. measurement\_record
40. threshold\_rule
41. alert\_rule
42. alert\_event
43. incident

## Wave 6 — Inventory and procurement

44. item\_category
45. item\_master
46. inventory\_location
47. inventory\_lot
48. stock\_balance
49. stock\_transaction
50. purchase\_request
51. purchase\_order
52. goods\_receipt

## Wave 7 — Packhouse and commerce

53. intake\_receipt
54. intake\_line
55. processing\_run
56. processing\_output
57. customer
58. sales\_order
59. sales\_order\_line
60. dispatch
61. dispatch\_line

## Wave 8 — SOP and compliance

62. sop\_document
63. sop\_link
64. training\_module
65. training\_completion
66. compliance\_record

## Wave 9 — Analytics and configuration

67. kpi\_definition
68. kpi\_snapshot
69. dashboard\_definition
70. dashboard\_widget
71. custom\_field\_definition
72. audit\_log

Note:

- `sop_document` and `task_template.required_sop_id` create a dependency issue.
- For clean migration order, either:
	- create `task_template.required_sop_id` nullable in a later migration, or
		- move `sop_document` before task\_template.

Best quality path: **add `required_sop_id` in a later alter migration**.

---

# 9\. Seed data order

## Seed 1

- permission
- system roles
- location\_type
- production\_system\_type
- species
- life\_stage
- asset\_category
- sensor\_type
- item\_category

## Seed 2

- one demo organization
- organization settings
- admin user
- admin membership
- role assignments
- one demo site

## Seed 3

- demo location tree
- demo production units
- demo workflows
- demo task templates
- demo shifts

## Seed 4

- demo item masters
- demo suppliers
- demo customers
- demo dashboard definitions
- demo KPI definitions

---

# 10\. Backend action ownership

This matters because agents should not expose raw CRUD and call it done.

## Tenant & Identity actions

- invite\_member
- activate\_member
- suspend\_member
- assign\_role
- revoke\_role

## Location actions

- create\_site
- add\_location\_node
- move\_location\_node
- deactivate\_location\_node

## Workflow actions

- create\_workflow
- version\_workflow
- publish\_workflow
- simulate\_workflow

## Task actions

- assign\_task
- start\_task
- pause\_task
- complete\_task
- fail\_task
- skip\_task
- reassign\_task

## Inventory actions

- receive\_stock
- move\_stock
- reserve\_stock
- consume\_stock
- adjust\_stock
- dispatch\_stock

## Biology actions

- create\_crop\_cycle
- stock\_cohort
- transfer\_cohort
- record\_mortality
- record\_growth\_sample
- harvest\_batch

---

# 11\. Constraints for agents

## Forbidden

- creating duplicate batch concepts
- hardcoding greenhouse-only structures
- creating page-led schema
- bypassing organization boundaries
- frontend-only permissions
- writing directly to audit tables from UI
- inventing ad hoc JSON blobs for core relational data

## Allowed

- JSONB for:
	- layout configs
		- workflow schemas
		- validation rules
		- metadata extensions
		- tenant custom config

## Change control

Any new core table after approval of v1 must include:

- purpose
- owning module
- why existing tables are insufficient
- FKs
- migration placement
- effect on audit and permissions

---

# 12\. What starts immediately after approval of A

After this schema package is approved, the correct next move is:

## Build Package 01 — Core Foundation

Includes:

- wave 1 migrations
- seed 1 and seed 2
- tenant isolation tests
- role/permission guard middleware
- audit logging hooks
- base admin APIs

Then:

## Build Package 02 — Structure + Workflow Core

Includes:

- wave 2 and wave 3 migrations
- site/location tree APIs
- production unit APIs
- workflow definition/version APIs
- task instance APIs
- workflow publish logic

Then:

## Build Package 03 — Worker Operations MVP

Includes:

- task assignment
- my tasks
- task completion
- exception reporting
- supervisor workboard

---

# 13\. My recommendation after this document

The next thing to do is **not** more theory.

The next deliverable should be:

**Build Package 01 spec**  
with:

- exact tables to create
- exact indexes
- seed content
- API endpoints
- tests
- acceptance criteria
- owner agent assignments

That is what turns this from design into controlled construction.

I recommend we do that next.

give me a pdf of all the information needed as resourses in my future chats

4/4

#### You said:

Output the summary structured by these categories, ensuring all relevant information is placed in the appropriate section

  

#### ChatGPT said:

## Business

- No direct business operations, revenue model, pricing, sales process, or commercial credentials were defined in this conversation.
- The platform direction discussed implies a future software business around Terra as a farm operating system, but no formal business model was finalized here.

## Business Goals

- Prioritize **project stability and quality over speed**.
- Avoid shortcuts and prevent architectural drift before code generation begins.
- Ensure agents build Terra **systematically from controlled plans**, not by randomly generating code.
- Establish a high-discipline development process where architecture, schema, migrations, permissions, and dependencies are fixed before broad implementation.

## Family

- No family information was discussed in this conversation.

## Farm

- Terra is intended to support a broad agricultural operating environment, including:
	- hydroponics
		- aquaponics
		- aquaculture
		- nursery
		- packhouse
		- procurement
		- inventory
		- dispatch
		- SOP/training/compliance
		- customizable workflows
		- customizable dashboards
		- multi-tenant permissions
- Physical farm/location structures Terra must support include:
	- farms
		- greenhouses
		- tunnels
		- nurseries
		- fish systems
		- packhouses
		- stores
		- cold rooms
		- dispatch areas
		- processing areas
- Example location path logic required:
	- `Farm A → Tunnel Block A → Tunnel 4 → DWC Zone B → Row 3`
- Production system categories explicitly planned:
	- hydroponic NFT
		- hydroponic DWC
		- hydroponic drip
		- hydroponic vertical tower
		- nursery
		- aquaculture RAS
		- aquaponics
		- mushroom
		- poultry layer
		- goat
		- cattle
		- soil field
		- packhouse
- Biological tracking planned for:
	- crops
		- seedlings
		- fish
		- prawns
		- eggs
		- livestock
- Environmental data support planned for:
	- pH
		- EC
		- dissolved oxygen
		- air temperature
		- water temperature
		- humidity
		- flow rate
		- pressure
		- water level
		- ammonia
		- nitrite
		- nitrate
		- CO2
		- light intensity

## Memory

- User requested a reusable future-chat resource summary and a categorized summary format for retention/handover.
- The conversation established several “carry-forward” truths that should be preserved for future chats:
	- Terra must be built systematically, not by ad hoc UI/code generation.
		- Stability and quality are more important than speed.
		- Option A (schema package first) was chosen as the correct quality-first path.
		- After Schema Package v1, the next phase should be Build Package 01 — Core Foundation.
		- The schema package is intended to become the controlled source of truth before broad coding begins.

## Projects

### Terra / YBATerra platform planning

- The user requested creation of an **OpenClaw Agent Task Plan** so agents can construct Terra systematically.
- A structured build plan of approximately **100 tasks** was defined.
- The purpose of the plan is to prevent agents from randomly generating code and instead force ordered construction.

### 100-task OpenClaw build structure

The build plan was organized into 10 phases:

#### Phase 1 — Foundation and build rules (Tasks 1–10)

- Monorepo structure
- Coding standards and contribution rules
- Canonical product architecture document
- Environment configuration system
- Logging and error handling standards
- Automated testing pipeline
- CI pipeline
- CD/release skeleton
- Shared design tokens and UI primitives
- Feature flag framework

#### Phase 2 — Core tenancy and identity (Tasks 11–20)

- Multi-tenant data model
- Organization onboarding model
- Authentication integration
- User profile model
- Role model
- Permission matrix engine
- Organization membership APIs
- Admin-only permission UI
- Tenant isolation tests
- Audit log for auth/role changes

#### Phase 3 — Master entity model (Tasks 21–30)

- Master location hierarchy
- Production system taxonomy
- Asset model
- Crop master model
- Aquaculture species master
- Livestock species master
- Batch/lot model
- Unit-of-measure framework
- Status/state framework
- Seed master data

#### Phase 4 — Workflow engine and configurability (Tasks 31–40)

- Workflow engine schema
- Workflow builder backend
- Workflow builder UI
- Task template system
- Task dependency management
- Recurrence/scheduling rules
- Workflow versioning
- Workflow permissions
- Workflow simulation/test mode
- Workflow execution audit trail

#### Phase 5 — Worker operations layer (Tasks 41–50)

- Worker-focused UI shell
- My Tasks screen
- Location-guided task instructions
- Task completion flow
- Supervisor reassignment flow
- Offline-capable task cache plan
- Worker KPI snippets
- Shift/day workboard
- Exception reporting flow
- Acknowledgment and checklist controls

#### Phase 6 — Dashboard and analytics framework (Tasks 51–60)

- Dashboard widget framework
- Customizable dashboard layout engine
- Admin dashboard widgets
- Worker dashboard widgets
- Supervisor dashboard widgets
- Operations dashboard widgets
- Environment dashboard widgets
- Dashboard permissions
- Saved dashboard presets
- Dashboard performance optimization

#### Phase 7 — Hydroponics and nursery domain (Tasks 61–70)

- Nursery production model
- Crop cycle model for hydroponics
- Hydroponic structures
- Planting/transplant recording
- Nutrient and dosing events
- Irrigation/water management events
- Crop observation model
- Harvest event flow
- Crop loss and waste recording
- Hydroponic KPI calculations

#### Phase 8 — Aquaculture domain (Tasks 71–80)

- Aquaculture facility model
- Fish/prawn cohort model
- Stocking and transfer flows
- Feed schedule and feed event model
- Mortality and culling flow
- Water quality logging
- Growth sampling
- Treatment/quarantine
- Harvest and processing handoff
- Aquaculture KPI calculations

#### Phase 9 — Packhouse, inventory, procurement, commerce (Tasks 81–90)

- Packhouse intake model
- Grading/washing/packing workflow model
- Finished goods inventory
- Raw materials inventory
- Procurement request and PO flow
- Supplier master model
- Customer master model
- Sales order model
- Dispatch and delivery flow
- Inventory and commerce KPIs

#### Phase 10 — SOP, compliance, alerts, integrations, readiness (Tasks 91–100)

- SOP and knowledge base model
- Training assignment model
- Compliance record model
- Alert and incident engine
- Notification routing framework
- Integration contract layer
- Reporting/export layer
- Platform observability layer
- Staging seed environment
- Final platform hardening pass

### Agent operating brief

The agent rules established were:

1. Build only from the numbered task list.
2. Complete dependencies before higher layers.
3. Every task must output code, tests, migrations, and notes.
4. No invented pages or tables outside the approved model.
5. Reuse shared entities and UI primitives.
6. All write actions must be permission-checked.
7. All critical actions must be audit-logged.
8. Prefer modular, replaceable architecture over shortcuts.
9. Mark blockers explicitly rather than guessing.
10. Update task state using: not started, in progress, blocked, done.

### Critical dependency rules

The following were declared non-negotiable:

- Do not build feature screens before the entity model exists.
- Do not build task execution before permissions exist.
- Do not build dashboards before KPI definitions exist.
- Do not build configurable workflows before workflow versioning exists.
- Do not build production modules without batch/lot traceability.
- Do not build alerts without an incident audit trail.
- Do not connect AI/agents directly to production write actions without permission gates and logs.

### Recommended initial startup tasks

The best immediate starting set was defined as:

- 1 Monorepo structure
- 2 Coding standards
- 3 Product architecture doc
- 4 Environment configuration
- 5 Logging/error handling
- 6 Testing pipeline
- 11 Multi-tenant model
- 14 User profiles
- 15 Roles
- 16 Permissions
- 21 Location hierarchy
- 31 Workflow engine schema

### Spreadsheet artifact

- A spreadsheet artifact was created:
	- **OpenClaw\_Terra\_Agent\_Build\_Sheet.xlsx**
- It included:
	- Overview
		- 100-task register
		- Dependency map
		- 8-week launch queue

### Immediate execution guidance after spreadsheet

The immediate execution guidance defined was:

1. Lock stack and repo structure.
2. Assign owner agents for the first 12 startup tasks.
3. Start Week 1 tasks: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6.
4. Do not start UI-heavy work until 11, 14, 15, 16, 21, and 31 are underway.
5. Use four task states only: Not Started, In Progress, Blocked, Done.
6. Require code, tests, notes, and status updates for every task.
7. Use the launch queue sheet as first 8-week execution order.

### Layered execution order

The agent execution order was defined as:

- Layer 1: Platform Foundation — Tasks 1–20 — estimated 2 weeks
- Layer 2: Core Engine — Tasks 31–40 — estimated 1 week
- Layer 3: Operations Interface — Tasks 41–50 — estimated 1 week
- Layer 4: Dashboards — Tasks 51–60 — estimated 1 week
- Layer 5: Production Domains — Tasks 61–80 — estimated 2 weeks
- Layer 6: Commerce + Compliance — Tasks 81–100 — estimated 2 weeks

### Timeline expectations

- First usable Terra prototype: **6–8 weeks**
- Production grade: **4–6 months**

### Terra positioning

The platform was explicitly positioned as:

- a **Farm Operating System**
- not just a farm management app
- comparable in category to:
	- ERP for agriculture
		- production OS
		- traceability engine
		- automation platform

### Terra Master Data Model blueprint

A full master data model blueprint was created, organized into 12 domains:

1. Tenant and Identity
2. Location and Physical Structure
3. Production Taxonomy
4. Biological Entities
5. Operational Work
6. Assets and Infrastructure
7. Environment and Measurements
8. Inventory and Materials
9. Packhouse and Commerce
10. SOP, Training, and Compliance
11. Alerts, Incidents, and Audit
12. Analytics and Configuration

### Core design rules from the master data model

1. Every record belongs to an organization.
2. Most operational records link to a site and often a specific location node.
3. Anything produced, moved, treated, harvested, packed, or sold should have a batch/lot/cohort identity.
4. Every action should be traceable to who, when, where, and what.
5. No workflow should exist without linking to a real business object.
6. Configuration must be tenant-aware.

### Key master data entities defined

Major entities defined in blueprint form included:

- organization
- organization settings
- user
- organization membership
- role
- permission
- role permission
- user role assignment
- worker profile
- site
- location type
- location node
- location alias
- production system type
- production unit
- season/production calendar
- species
- variety/cultivar/strain
- life stage
- crop cycle
- animal/aquatic cohort
- biological batch/lot
- workflow definition
- workflow version
- workflow stage
- task template
- workflow task definition
- task instance
- task event
- shift
- asset category
- asset
- asset relationship
- maintenance plan
- maintenance event
- sensor type
- sensor device
- measurement record
- setpoint/threshold rule
- item category
- item master
- inventory location
- inventory lot
- stock balance
- stock transaction
- supplier
- purchase request
- purchase order
- goods receipt
- intake receipt
- intake line
- processing run
- processing output
- customer
- sales order
- sales order line
- dispatch
- dispatch line
- SOP document
- SOP link
- training module
- training completion
- compliance record
- alert rule
- alert event
- incident
- audit log
- KPI definition
- KPI snapshot
- dashboard definition
- dashboard widget
- custom field definition

### Recommended database build waves from master data model

#### Wave 1 — Absolute foundation

- Organization
- Organization Settings
- User
- Organization Membership
- Role
- Permission
- User Role Assignment
- Site
- Location Type
- Location Node
- Production System Type
- Production Unit
- Species
- Variety
- Life Stage
- Asset Category
- Asset
- Workflow Definition
- Workflow Version
- Task Template
- Task Instance
- Audit Log

#### Wave 2 — Operational backbone

- Crop Cycle
- Animal/Aquatic Cohort
- Biological Batch
- Task Event
- Sensor Type
- Sensor Device
- Measurement Record
- Alert Rule
- Alert Event
- Incident

#### Wave 3 — Inventory and commerce

- Item Category
- Item Master
- Inventory Location
- Inventory Lot
- Stock Balance
- Stock Transaction
- Supplier
- Purchase Request
- Purchase Order
- Goods Receipt
- Customer
- Sales Order
- Dispatch

#### Wave 4 — Compliance and analytics

- Training Module
- Training Completion
- Compliance Record
